messages.content:
I have this:
<Style x:Key="MyStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridA}">
        <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource DataGridColumnHeaderStyle}"/>
    </Style>

I want to add the same style to a DataGridB, how can I do that?

You can create a base style for both DataGridA and DataGridB, and then derive specific styles from the base style for each DataGrid. Here's an example:

Create a base style without any specific target type:

```xml
<Style x:Key="BaseDataGridStyle" TargetType="DataGrid">
    <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource DataGridColumnHeaderStyle}"/>
    <!-- other common setters for DataGrid -->
</Style>
```

Then, derive a specific style for DataGridA using the BasedOn attribute:

```xml
<Style x:Key="DataGridAStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridA}" BasedOn="{StaticResource BaseDataGridStyle}">
    <!-- additional setters for DataGridA -->
</Style>
```

Finally, derive a specific style for DataGridB using the BasedOn attribute:

```xml
<Style x:Key="DataGridBStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridB}" BasedOn="{StaticResource BaseDataGridStyle}">
    <!-- additional setters for DataGridB -->
</Style>
```

Now, you can apply the DataGridAStyle to DataGridA, and DataGridBStyle to DataGridB:

```xml
<local:DataGridA Style="{StaticResource DataGridAStyle}"/>
<local:DataGridB Style="{StaticResource DataGridBStyle}"/>
```
I can't use this <Style x:Key=â€śBaseDataGridStyleâ€ť TargetType=â€śDataGridâ€ť> because I don't want to overwrite the default DataGrid, "mydatagrid" is my own class with my own implementation

In that case, you can create a base style with a `TargetType` of your custom `DataGrid` class `MyDataGrid`, and then use the `BasedOn` property to derive specific styles for `DataGridA` and `DataGridB`.

Here's an example:
```xml
<Style x:Key="BaseMyDataGridStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:MyDataGrid}">
    <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource DataGridColumnHeaderStyle}"/>
    <!-- other common setters for MyDataGrid -->
</Style>

<Style x:Key="DataGridAStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridA}" BasedOn="{StaticResource BaseMyDataGridStyle}">
    <!-- additional setters for DataGridA -->
</Style>

<Style x:Key="DataGridBStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridB}" BasedOn="{StaticResource BaseMyDataGridStyle}">
    <!-- additional setters for DataGridB -->
</Style>
```

Then, you can apply the specific styles to the corresponding `DataGrid` instances like this:

```xml
<local:DataGridA Style="{StaticResource DataGridAStyle}" />
<local:DataGridB Style="{StaticResource DataGridBStyle}" />
```

Note that this assumes you have defined your `DataGridA` and `DataGridB` custom classes as subclasses of `MyDataGrid`. If your custom `DataGrid` classes don't use the same inheritance hierarchy, you may need to adjust the `TargetType` of the styles accordingly.

I can't use <Style x:Key=â€śBaseMyDataGridStyleâ€ť TargetType=â€ś{x:Type local:MyDataGrid}â€ť> because MyDatagrid is generic, and DataGridA and DataGridB are implementations like this: DataGridA : MyDataGrid<A> and DataGridB : MyDataGrid<B>.

In that case, you can make the base style's `TargetType` a non-generic type that both `DataGridA` and `DataGridB` inherit from, and use the `BasedOn` property to derive specific styles for each of them. 

For example, if `DataGridA` and `DataGridB` inherit from a non-generic base class called `MyBaseDataGrid`, you can create a base style targeted to `MyBaseDataGrid` and then derive specific styles for `DataGridA` and `DataGridB` like this:
```xml
<Style x:Key="BaseMyBaseDataGridStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:MyBaseDataGrid}">
    <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource DataGridColumnHeaderStyle}"/>
    <!-- other common setters for MyBaseDataGrid -->
</Style>

<Style x:Key="DataGridAStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridA}" BasedOn="{StaticResource BaseMyBaseDataGridStyle}">
    <!-- additional setters for DataGridA -->
</Style>

<Style x:Key="DataGridBStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridB}" BasedOn="{StaticResource BaseMyBaseDataGridStyle}">
    <!-- additional setters for DataGridB -->
</Style>
```

Then, apply the specific styles to the corresponding `DataGrid` instances like this:
```xml
<local:DataGridA Style="{StaticResource DataGridAStyle}" />
<local:DataGridB Style="{StaticResource DataGridBStyle}" />
```

Note that the exact base class you use for `MyBaseDataGrid` will depend on your specific implementation details. The main point is to use a non-generic base class that both `DataGridA` and `DataGridB` inherit from, and target the base style to that base class.

I did this, but it gives me error: Exception: You can only base on a Style whose target type is the base type 'DataGridBFilter'.

<Style x:Key="DataGridAStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridAFilter}">
        <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource DataGridColumnHeaderStyle}"/>
    </Style>

<Style x:Key="GataGridBStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridBFilter}" BasedOn="{StaticResource DataGridAStyle}"/>

It looks like there's a typo in your second style definition. You have `GataGridBStyle` instead of `DataGridBStyle`. This may be causing the error.

Try fixing the typo and see if the error goes away:
```xml
<Style x:Key="DataGridAStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridAFilter}">
    <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource DataGridColumnHeaderStyle}"/>
</Style>

<Style x:Key="DataGridBStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridBFilter}" BasedOn="{StaticResource DataGridAStyle}" />
```

If the error persists, it's possible that `local:DataGridBFilter` is not a subclass of `local:DataGridAFilter`, which would prevent you from basing the `DataGridBStyle` style on `DataGridAStyle`. In that case, you may need to create a base style for both `DataGridAFilter` and `DataGridBFilter`, rather than trying to derive `DataGridBStyle` from `DataGridAStyle`.
no it is not a subclass, they both inherit from the same parent which is a generic class DataGridFilter<T>

In that case, you can create a base style for `DataGridFilter<T>` that applies to both `DataGridAFilter` and `DataGridBFilter`, and then derive specific styles for each of them.

Here's an example:
```xml
<Style x:Key="BaseDataGridFilterStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridFilter}">
    <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource DataGridColumnHeaderStyle}"/>
    <!-- other common setters for DataGridFilter<T> -->
</Style>

<Style x:Key="DataGridAStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridAFilter}" BasedOn="{StaticResource BaseDataGridFilterStyle}">
    <!-- additional setters for DataGridAFilter -->
</Style>

<Style x:Key="DataGridBStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridBFilter}" BasedOn="{StaticResource BaseDataGridFilterStyle}">
    <!-- additional setters for DataGridBFilter -->
</Style>
```

Then, apply the specific styles to the corresponding `DataGrid` instances like this:
```xml
<local:DataGridAFilter Style="{StaticResource DataGridAStyle}" />
<local:DataGridBFilter Style="{StaticResource DataGridBStyle}" />
```

This way, both `DataGridAFilter` and `DataGridBFilter` will inherit the common style defined in `BaseDataGridFilterStyle`. The specific styles, `DataGridAStyle` and `DataGridBStyle`, can be used to customize the appearance of each of them.
it says it doesn't exist because it is a generic and it is a WPF

In that case, you can try defining a non-generic base class for your `DataGridFilter<T>` class and using that as the target type for your base style. Then, you can derive styles for both `DataGridAFilter` and `DataGridBFilter` based on this base style.

For example, suppose you define a non-generic `DataGridFilter` base class that both `DataGridAFilter` and `DataGridBFilter` inherit from:
```c#
public class DataGridFilter {
    // Common implementation for DataGridFilter
}

public class DataGridAFilter : DataGridFilter<A> {
    // Additional implementation for DataGridAFilter
}

public class DataGridBFilter : DataGridFilter<B> {
    // Additional implementation for DataGridBFilter
}
```

Then, you can define a base style for `DataGridFilter` and derive specific styles for `DataGridAFilter` and `DataGridBFilter` based on this base style:
```xml
<Style x:Key="BaseDataGridFilterStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridFilter}">
    <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource DataGridColumnHeaderStyle}" />
    <!-- other common setters for DataGridFilter -->
</Style>

<Style x:Key="DataGridAStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridAFilter}" BasedOn="{StaticResource BaseDataGridFilterStyle}">
    <!-- additional setters for DataGridAFilter -->
</Style>

<Style x:Key="DataGridBStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridBFilter}" BasedOn="{StaticResource BaseDataGridFilterStyle}">
    <!-- additional setters for DataGridBFilter -->
</Style>
```

Then, apply the specific styles to the corresponding `DataGrid` instances like this:
```xml
<local:DataGridAFilter Style="{StaticResource DataGridAStyle}" />
<local:DataGridBFilter Style="{StaticResource DataGridBStyle}" />
```

This way, both `DataGridAFilter` and `DataGridBFilter` will inherit the common style defined in `BaseDataGridFilterStyle`. The specific styles, `DataGridAStyle` and `DataGridBStyle`, can be used to customize the appearance of each of them.
no this code is wrong, if you define a class as a non generic you can't inherit it as a generic like this DataGridFilter<A>. Try looking ofr a different approach

I apologize for the confusion. In that case, you can keep the `DataGridFilter<T>` class as a generic class and create a base style using a generic type parameter.

Here's an example:
```xml
<Style x:Key="BaseDataGridFilterStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridFilter`1}">
    <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource DataGridColumnHeaderStyle}"/>
    <!-- other common setters for DataGridFilter<T> -->
</Style>

<Style x:Key="DataGridAStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridAFilter}" BasedOn="{StaticResource BaseDataGridFilterStyle}">
    <!-- additional setters for DataGridAFilter -->
</Style>

<Style x:Key="DataGridBStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridBFilter}" BasedOn="{StaticResource BaseDataGridFilterStyle}">
    <!-- additional setters for DataGridBFilter -->
</Style>
```

Note that the `TargetType` of the `BaseDataGridFilterStyle` style is now `local:DataGridFilter` followed by a backtick and the generic type parameter `1` to specify that it applies to `DataGridFilter<T>` where `T` is a single generic type parameter.

Then, apply the specific styles to the corresponding `DataGrid` instances like this:
```xml
<local:DataGridAFilter Style="{StaticResource DataGridAStyle}" />
<local:DataGridBFilter Style="{StaticResource DataGridBStyle}" />
```

This should allow you to create a base style that applies to both `DataGridAFilter` and `DataGridBFilter`. The specific styles, `DataGridAStyle` and `DataGridBStyle`, can be used to customize the appearance of each of them.
it says DataGridFilter1 doesn't exist

I apologize for my mistake. The correct syntax for the generic type name in XAML is `x:TypeArguments`, not a backtick followed by the number of type arguments. 

Here's how your base style should look like with the correct syntax:
```xml
<Style x:Key="BaseDataGridFilterStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridFilter}" x:TypeArguments="x:Boolean">
    <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource DataGridColumnHeaderStyle}"/>
    <!-- other common setters for DataGridFilter<T> -->
</Style>
```
Note that the `x:TypeArguments` attribute is set to `x:Boolean`, which means the `DataGridFilter` class is being parameterized with a single Boolean type argument.

Then, derive specific styles for `DataGridAFilter` and `DataGridBFilter` using the `BasedOn` attribute with the base style:
```xml
<Style x:Key="DataGridAStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridAFilter}" BasedOn="{StaticResource BaseDataGridFilterStyle}">
    <!-- additional setters for DataGridAFilter -->
</Style>

<Style x:Key="DataGridBStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:DataGridBFilter}" BasedOn="{StaticResource BaseDataGridFilterStyle}">
    <!-- additional setters for DataGridBFilter -->
</Style>
```
And finally, apply the specific styles to the corresponding `DataGrid` instances:
```xml
<local:DataGridAFilter Style="{StaticResource DataGridAStyle}" />
<local:DataGridBFilter Style="{StaticResource DataGridBStyle}" />
```

I hope this resolves the issue for you.

ConsumoPersonal itemConsumo = new ConsumoPersonal

                            Detalle = sqliteDataReader.GetString(5),
                            Consumidor = MainWindow.BuscarOperador(sqliteDataReader.GetInt32(6)),
                        };
